option_desc: A grassroots organisation built around a specific community or cause.
A research body or university lab focused on advancing knowledge.
A publicly funded body accountable to citizens.
A fast-moving company that expects rapid growth.
A dominant technology platform with enormous resources and global reach.
Prioritises the rights of individuals and communities over speed or profit.
Decisions are made carefully, openly and are reviewed by stakeholders.
Believes technology is inherently good and that most problems will be solved by building more and better tools.
Ethical questions are only dealt with when something goes wrong publicly.
Problems are only addressed when they become a legal or reputational threat.
Funded by public money through government bodies or research councils.
Users pay a regular fee to access the service.
Users are charged each time they use the service.
Funded by borrowing a large amount of money that must be repaid with interest.
Funded by a single wealthy individual or corporation.
Text collected from across the internet, including news sites, blogs, forums, and wikis.
Published novels, non-fiction, journalism, and other written works. Generally well-written and edited, but less diversity than other options.
Peer-reviewed research papers, theses, and scientific literature.
Data generated specifically with or extracted from the organisation's own users and tasks.
Posts, comments, videos, and conversations from platforms like Twitter, TikTok, Reddit, and Facebook.
The organisation creates or gathers its own data directly, with full control over quality and consent.
Data bought or formally licensed from whoever owns it.
Gathered from paid workers or volunteers who contribute data knowingly.
Publicly available datasets released under open licences by researchers or institutions.
Artificial data generated by other AI models or simulations.
Collected by automated tools that crawl websites and download content without asking permission.
The model finds patterns in raw data with no human guidance. Cheaper, but might lack accuracy.
People review and score the model's outputs, and those scores are used to steer its behaviour.
A general-purpose model is retrained on a smaller, carefully chosen dataset for a specific job.

The model runs on the user's own phone, laptop, or local hardware. Maximum privacy because data never leaves the device, but only small models can run this way and performance depends on the user's hardware.
The organisation runs the model on its own servers and hardware.
The model is spread across many independent computers run by different people or groups.
The model is accessed through a big tech company's services e.g. OpenAI, Google, or Microsoft.
Tiny and fast; can only be used for very basic tasks.
Works reasonably well for simple tasks like summarising.
Can perform more complex functions like basic reasoning.
Can do complex tasks like coding.
The most powerful models in the world. Can tackle difficult problems like building a whole website from scratch.
The model is instructed to be cautious above all else. It will refuse requests it considers risky but may refuse to help with reasonable tasks.
The model has guardrails for clearly harmful requests but tries to be as helpful as possible within those limits.
Users can set their own rules for how the model behaves, (including removing safety measures).
